# 9 best practices for ESG reporting & strategy

![An ESG team discussing ESG best practices](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcdn.sanity.io%2Fimages%2F33u1mixi%2Fproduction%2Fa741d8dc206eda89801b3b89b0cceda7c9838b38-1080x720.jpg&w=2048&q=75)

[Environmental, social and governance (ESG)](https://www.diligent.com/resources/guides/esg) issues are top-of-mind for many corporations. In fact, a recent survey showed that [43% of shareholders](https://www.nysscpa.org/news/publications/the-trusted-professional/article/survey-majority-of-corporate-directors-see-esg-reporting-as-essential-many-take-steps-toward-board-diversity) want to talk to boards about ESG — more than any other topic on the board agenda. As a result, many corporations are scrambling to identify and implement ESG best practices: the set of policies and protocols that make up an effective ESG program.

At the same time, corporate boards are becoming more [willing to disclose](https://www.wsj.com/articles/corporate-boards-are-disclosing-more-on-diversity-of-directors-esg-insight-d6c1f17a) ESG-related information, like board diversity. This signals a focus on ESG reporting practices as a key way corporations will double down on their efforts now and in the future. To help you keep up, this article will explain:

## Why is effective ESG reporting important?

[ESG reporting](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-reporting) is essential to risk management. Corporations face more climate risk than ever, and consumers worldwide are holding companies to a higher standard, causing boards to look to ESG as a way to adapt to this new normal. Many institutions and investors have also [prioritized ESG](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/why-is-esg-important) in recent years, giving corporations a powerful [financial motivation](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/sustainable-finance) to commit to ESG.

## Our 9 ESG best practices

### 1. Assemble an ESG team

ESG is complex, and it’s more than implementing a recycling program or using compostable packing for your products. So, while it may be tempting to have team members double duty as ESG advocates alongside their current roles, it’s a critical ESG best practice to create a dedicated team.

This can include a sustainability consultant, a diversity consultant, a risk manager dedicated to ESG risks and more. Be sure to prioritize [diversity](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/diversity); it’s not only essential to the “S” in ESG, but it’s also the best way to tap into the many experiences and backgrounds it takes to address ESG effectively.

### 2. Choose an ESG framework

Thousands of ESG regulations are on the books, but not all may be relevant to your specific ESG reporting needs. Choosing the right framework remains an ESG best practice because it’s essential to setting your program up for success.

Organizations that create a framework themselves can too often misconstrue their ESG performance by simply disclosing how effective they are at the priorities they’ve identified on their own. Adhering to an accepted framework, however, shows that you’re committed to a more holistic view of ESG performance — one that’s tied as much to the world around you as it is to your objectives.

Some of the most common ESG reporting frameworks include:

### 3. Set ESG goals

It’s critical that you set [ESG goals](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-goals) to serve as your program’s north star. These goals should be related to the framework you selected. If you selected [GRI](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/gri-standards), for example, you might prioritize areas the framework already emphasizes: your [GHG emissions](https://www.diligent.com/insights/esg/how-to-measure-greenhouse-gas-emissions/), waste attributed to your production, how safe your facilities are, etc.

This ESG best practice will help you make intentional, strategic changes that will reduce your impact on the environment and your community. It also makes it easier to disclose the results of your ESG efforts.

### 4. Identify metrics for your goals

Goals alone aren’t enough. That’s because your ESG goals are broad statements about what you hope to achieve. Metrics, on the other hand, are the specific data points you’ll use to measure your progress. For example, if pay equity is your goal a key metric could be CEO pay ratios.

As you research and select data points, [metrics](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/metrics) that meet our ESG best practices are those that are:

### 5. Collect ESG data

[ESG data](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-data-management) is central to your efforts. Many corporations engage in what’s called greenwashing, which is an attempt to appear committed to ESG without taking any action. Corporations with clear reporting and data can easily prove that their efforts are genuine and contribute to meaningful, measurable impact.

It’s an ESG best practice to not only collect and centralize data related to your ESG metrics but to translate that information into an [ESG score](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-risk-scores). Your ESG score will indicate your risks and opportunities, the efficacy of your initiatives and how effectively the board is managing ESG.

### 6. Strategize on improvements

In ESG reporting, metrics should be actionable. If the metrics show that you have yet to meet your ESG goals, consider how you can adapt your strategy to make more progress. If you have met your goals, consider redefining that goal or setting a new one, pivoting to ESG areas you have yet to emphasize.

It’s important to focus on improvements that will meaningfully impact your business. If, for example, you ship products overseas, the ESG best practice is to create a strategy that reduces *those* emissions — not just the electricity usage at your company’s headquarters.

### 7. Create visibility around ESG performance

Investors, regulators and the board want visibility into your ESG program. For this reason, ESG reporting isn’t only about creating internal benchmarks. It’s translating those benchmarks into clear, centralized reports that prove your commitment to investors, prove your compliance to regulators and prove the value of ESG to your board.

ESG reporting best practices state that you have a single source of truth for all reporting, that your reports offer real-time insights and that dashboards are clear and easy to interpret.

### 8. Monitor ESG

ESG issues rapidly evolve. [Policy](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/policy) and industry leaders update frameworks and expectations. New ESG priorities take hold among investors. The board may ask for visibility into ESG areas they consider important for the corporation’s future.

Each of these scenarios, and more, can impact the success of your ESG program. It’s also possible that your [ESG strategy](https://www.diligent.com/resources/blog/esg-strategy) won’t perform as expected. In either case, monitoring is how you can identify problems before they develop into larger threats. It’s a best practice to consider ESG an always-on function and closely monitor regulatory, political and shareholder updates and your own ESG data to ensure your efforts don’t fall behind.

### 9. Utilize ESG technology

ESG may be motivated by a desire to protect people and the planet, but in reality, it’s heavily influenced by data. From emissions to waste, biodiversity to human rights, and so much more, corporations are expected to track and centralize numerous metrics and then use those metrics to prove their continued commitment to sustainability.

Manually setting goals, tracking metrics and reporting on ESG is time-consuming. ESG technology can automate much of that process by defining key indicators, reporting on those indicators, and then centralizing your data into a single source of truth. This is the best way to create an ESG program that’s actionable for the board and defensible to investors and regulators.

## Lead your industry on ESG

Modern ESG is still a relatively new function, which means there’s an opportunity to lead the way on ESG best practices. Using the right solutions can signal to investors and regulators that you take ESG seriously. Those same solutions can also equip your board with the clear and credible insights they need to make informed decisions about ESG.

Diligent ESG helps generate accurate sustainability reports by automating data collection and creating thorough, audit-ready reports. See how [Diligent ESG](https://www.diligent.com/solutions/carbon-accounting) can be the single source of truth your organization needs to chart a path forward on ESG.

Best practices in ESG reporting: enhancing corporate responsibility
===================================================================

12 minute read ⏱

 Last update: May 2025

Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting is a cornerstone of modern corporate responsibility. As sustainability and ethical governance become increasingly critical for businesses, ESG reporting helps organizations showcase their commitment to addressing societal challenges, reducing environmental impact, and ensuring strong governance practices.

More than just a compliance exercise, effective ESG reporting enables companies to build stronger relationships with stakeholders, enhance transparency, and contribute to long-term financial performance.

With mounting pressure from regulatory frameworks like the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the increasing expectations of investors and consumers, companies must integrate ESG reporting into their core business strategy.

This article will cover best practices in ESG reporting, focusing on how businesses can enhance their corporate responsibility, improve decision-making, and drive sustainability outcomes.

**************The importance of ESG reporting for corporate responsibility**************
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[ESG](https://www.imd.org/blog/governance/esg-policies/) reporting has become a vital tool for businesses to demonstrate their commitment to corporate responsibility and long-term sustainability. By providing transparency on a company’s environmental impact, social initiatives, and governance practices, ESG reporting builds trust with stakeholders and shows that the company is accountable for its actions.

By consistently communicating **ESG performance**, companies can enhance their reputation as ethical corporate citizens. Here’s why ESG reporting is so important to organizations in current times:

### **Improves transparency and trust**

Transparent ESG reporting allows companies to openly communicate their impact on key areas such as climate change, human rights, and [corporate governance](https://www.imd.org/blog/governance/what-is-corporate-governance/?ref=blog). This transparency strengthens stakeholder trust, which is essential for maintaining a positive reputation in the market.

Companies like Patagonia, for example, have built their brand on transparent ESG reporting, earning the loyalty of environmentally conscious consumers.

### **Addresses stakeholder concerns**

Investors, customers, employees, and communities increasingly expect companies to demonstrate their commitment to sustainability. ESG reporting directly addresses these concerns and shows how the business is responding to issues such as labor practices, [carbon emissions](https://www.imd.org/blog/sustainability/carbon-management/?ref=blog), and corporate governance.

### **Enhances social responsibility**

Social responsibility initiatives are a key component of ESG reporting. Companies can highlight their efforts to improve labor conditions, support [diversity and inclusion](https://www.imd.org/blog/management/diversity-in-the-workplace/?ref=blog), and engage with local communities. Starbucks, for example, is known for its robust reporting on social impact, including its ethical sourcing practices and diversity programs.

**************Common ESG reporting frameworks and standards**************
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

**FRAMEWORK****DESCRIPTION****EXAMPLES OF USAGE**
**_Global Reporting Initiative (GRI)_**A widely used global framework focusing on sustainability reporting, addressing climate change, human rights, and labor practices. Suitable for large multinational corporations.Used by companies like Unilever for comprehensive sustainability reporting.
**_Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB)_**Provides industry-specific standards for reporting financially material ESG topics, helping link ESG efforts to financial performance.Manufacturing companies may report on water usage, while tech companies report on data privacy and energy consumption.
**_Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD)_**Focuses on climate-related risks and opportunities, integrating climate issues into financial reporting. Crucial for high-emission industries.Shell uses it to report on risks related to the energy transition.
**_Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)_**European regulatory framework mandating large companies to disclose detailed sustainability information, impacting companies operating in Europe.Relevant for European companies that need to comply with sustainability disclosures.

Selecting the right ESG reporting framework is crucial for companies aiming to meet both regulatory requirements and stakeholder expectations. Several established frameworks guide businesses in measuring and reporting on their ESG performance, helping them ensure consistency, transparency, and comparability across industries.

Popular ESG reporting frameworks and standards include:

### **Global Reporting Initiative (GRI)**

One of the most widely used frameworks globally, GRI focuses on sustainability reporting and addresses topics such as climate change, human rights, and labor practices. The GRI standards are designed to be comprehensive and applicable across sectors, making them suitable for large multinational corporations like Unilever.

### **Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB)**

SASB is particularly useful for companies looking to report on financially material ESG topics. It provides industry-specific standards that help businesses link their ESG efforts directly to their financial performance. For example, SASB standards may guide a manufacturing company in reporting on water usage and pollution control, while a tech company might focus on data privacy and energy consumption.

### **Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD)**

Focused on climate-related risks and opportunities, TCFD helps companies integrate climate-related issues into their financial reporting. It’s particularly important for organizations in high-emission industries like energy and transport, where climate risks can have a significant financial impact.

For instance, Shell uses TCFD guidelines to report on how it is managing risks related to the global energy transition.

### **Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)**

As the new regulatory framework for European companies, [CSRD](https://www.imd.org/blog/governance/csrd/?ref=blog) mandates that large companies disclose more detailed information on sustainability, including their impact on the environment, social issues, and governance. This directive is set to affect thousands of companies, making it crucial for businesses operating in Europe to comply.

**Key ESG metrics and performance indicators**
----------------------------------------------

Identifying and tracking key ESG metrics is essential for companies looking to improve their sustainability performance and demonstrate accountability. Accurate data collection and analysis are critical to tracking these metrics effectively. Companies must ensure their ESG data is reliable and reflects real-world outcomes.

The following **performance indicators** help businesses measure progress in areas such as environmental impact, social equity, and governance practices.

### **Environmental metrics**

These performance indicators include:

*   Carbon emissions
*   Water usage
*   Waste management

Reducing carbon footprints and transitioning to renewable energy are key goals for many organizations. IKEA, for instance, tracks its carbon emissions to align with its goal of becoming carbon positive by 2030.

### **Social metrics**

These are indicators related to:

*   Labor practices
*   Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI)
*   Human rights

Companies should track progress in areas such as employee well-being, gender diversity, and supplier standards. Nike reports extensively on its labor practices within global supply chains, highlighting how it addresses worker safety and ethical sourcing.

### **Governance metrics**

Governance indicators focus on:

*   Board diversity
*   Executive compensation
*   Shareholder rights
*   Anti-corruption efforts

Governance metrics often involve linking executive compensation to ESG performance, ensuring that leadership is accountable for achieving sustainability goals. Microsoft, for instance, has tied executive bonuses to their sustainability objectives, reinforcing the importance of aligning leadership with long-term ESG performance.

**Best practices to strengthen ESG reporting for long-term success**
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Effective ESG reporting is about more than just meeting compliance requirements – it’s a powerful tool for identifying risks, improving decision-making, and aligning sustainability with your overall business strategy.

From managing key ESG risks to engaging stakeholders and reporting on climate change, companies need a clear approach to build trust and ensure transparency.

The following sections will guide you through essential best practices to enhance your ESG reporting, manage risks, align with business goals, and meet regulatory requirements. Whether it’s using data to inform decisions or effectively communicating sustainability efforts, these strategies will help you create a robust, impactful ESG reporting process.

### **1. Identify and manage key ESG risks**

**Materiality assessments** are essential for identifying the ESG issues that matter most to your business and stakeholders. Focusing on the most relevant risks helps improve the quality of your ESG reporting and strengthens your sustainability strategy.

Here’s how materiality assessments can help you manage key risks:

*   **Climate-related risks.**Many industries face significant risks due to climate change, such as extreme weather, regulatory changes, or shifting consumer demand. Using frameworks like TCFD, businesses can identify and report on these risks to mitigate their impact.
*   **Supply chain****risks.**Companies must assess risks like human rights violations, environmental damage, and legal compliance in their supply chains. Proactively managing these risks improves transparency.
*   **Governance risks.**Weak governance can lead to regulatory fines and reputational damage. Regularly conducting materiality assessments helps companies prioritize governance issues that affect both stakeholders and performance.

Materiality assessments help ensure that your ESG reporting is focused, relevant, and impactful by identifying and managing these critical risks.

### **2. Improve data collection**

Accurate ESG data collection is crucial for transparent and reliable ESG reporting. Without the right data, companies can’t effectively track their ESG performance or make informed decisions about sustainability efforts. Here are some strategies to enhance your data collection process:

*   **Leverage technology for data management.**Automating the ESG data collection process with specialized software can streamline reporting and ensure consistency. Platforms like Enablon and Datamaran help businesses collect, analyze, and report ESG data in real time, making the process more efficient.
*   **Utilize established****methodologies****.** Applying recognized methods like lifecycle assessments and carbon accounting standards ensures that your data accurately reflects your environmental and social impact. These methodologies add credibility to your ESG reports by providing robust and meaningful insights.
*   **Partner with external****providers****.**Collaborating with third-party ESG data providers can help verify the accuracy of your reports and ensure compliance with regulatory requirements. This approach allows businesses to improve decision-making and meet stakeholder expectations with reliable, verified data.

By following these practices, companies can develop a strong ESG reporting process that informs strategic decisions and drives continuous improvement.

### **3. Align ESG reporting with business strategy**

To maximize the effectiveness of ESG reporting, it must be tightly integrated with your company’s overall [business strategy](https://www.imd.org/blog/strategy/business-strategy/). When ESG goals align with core business objectives, companies can drive value creation, enhance decision-making, and achieve sustainable long-term growth.

**Here’s how to align ESG reporting with business strategy:**

*   **Incorporate ESG into****decision-making****.**Aligning ESG reporting with your business strategy allows for more informed decision-making that considers both sustainability risks and opportunities. This helps companies stay ahead of market shifts, meet regulatory demands, and remain competitive. For example, businesses that factor climate risks into their strategic planning can better mitigate financial impacts and seize growth opportunities.
*   **Tie****executive compensation****to****ESG performance****.** Follow the example of [Unilever](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2014-03-07/unilever-hands-ceo-polman-722-000-bonus-for-sustainability-work) – a company that increasingly links executive compensation to its ESG metrics. This ensures that leadership is accountable for driving sustainability efforts, making ESG a measurable, incentivized priority across the organization.
*   **Strengthen governance structures.**By aligning ESG reporting with corporate governance, businesses can ensure ESG considerations are embedded into operational and strategic decisions. Companies with robust governance structures are better positioned to manage stakeholder expectations and navigate complex regulatory requirements.

When ESG reporting is integrated into the overall business strategy, companies can create a growth roadmap that balances profitability with sustainability, ensuring both financial success and long-term resilience.

### **4. Engage stakeholders**

Engaging stakeholders in the ESG reporting process is essential for building trust and aligning your sustainability efforts with their expectations.

Here’s how to engage stakeholders effectively:

*   **Proactive communication.**Engage stakeholders early in the reporting process to identify their key concerns and expectations. This ensures your ESG reporting addresses the most relevant issues for both the company and its stakeholders.
*   **Transparent in reporting.**Keep stakeholders informed with regular updates on ESG performance. Transparent communication builds trust with shareholders, investors, and communities. For example, Danone has successfully maintained strong relationships by providing frequent updates on its sustainability efforts.

Proactively engaging stakeholders can help you create an ESG strategy that reflects their concerns and maintains transparency and accountability.

### **5. Include ESG initiatives and sustainability efforts**

Transparent reporting on ESG initiatives is critical to demonstrating the company’s progress toward sustainability goals. Whether it’s reducing carbon emissions, improving labor conditions, or enhancing community engagement, businesses need to effectively communicate their efforts to stakeholders.

Best practices for reporting ESG initiatives and sustainability efforts include:

*   **Being transparent about challenges and successes.**Companies should share both their achievements and the challenges they face in meeting their sustainability goals. For instance, IKEA regularly reports on its progress toward becoming a circular business but also highlights the difficulties in reaching this goal.
*   **Providing real-world examples.**Case studies and real-world examples of ESG initiatives in action help stakeholders understand the tangible impact of the company’s efforts. For example, Adidas frequently highlights its use of recycled material

ESG Compliance: Regulations, Risks, and Strategies for Businesses
=================================================================

[Back to Glossary Page](https://ecovadis.com/glossary/)

ESG compliance is no longer just a regulatory requirement – it’s a business imperative. Governments worldwide are tightening environmental, social, and governance regulations, investors are scrutinizing ESG performance, and consumers expect businesses to operate responsibly. Companies that fail to comply face legal penalties, supply chain disruptions, and loss of market access.

This guide breaks down what ESG compliance means, why it matters, and how businesses can meet evolving requirements. It covers key regulations across major markets, challenges companies face in implementation, and best practices for integrating ESG into corporate strategy.

What is ESG Compliance?
-----------------------

ESG compliance means following laws, regulations, and industry standards that cover environmental impact, social responsibility, and corporate governance. It requires businesses to track and report on areas like carbon emissions, labor rights, anti-bribery policies, and board oversight. It can be split into the three components that make up ESG:

*   **Environmental compliance:** Meeting emission reduction targets, following waste disposal laws, and improving energy efficiency. Many companies must report scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions under frameworks like the SEC Climate Disclosure Rule or the EU’s Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD).
*   **Social compliance:** Involves labor laws, supply chain due diligence, and human rights protections. Regulations like Germany’s Supply Chain Act (LkSG) and Canada’s Modern Slavery Act (Bill S-211) require businesses to monitor and prevent forced labor in their supply chains.
*   **Governance compliance:** Financial transparency, anti-corruption efforts, and board diversity. Companies must follow rules like the UK Bribery Act or the US Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) to prevent fraud and unethical business practices.

In addition to complying with laws and regulations, many organizations voluntarily comply with frameworks like GRI, SASB, and ISSB to meet investor expectations and improve sustainability reporting. The reason for this is simple: ESG compliance is now a core business requirement, affecting supply chain management, risk assessments, and investment decisions. It’s therefore good practice to tick as many boxes as possible.

Why ESG Compliance Matters
--------------------------

Indeed, as much as ESG compliance might feel like a box-ticking exercise, it’s about far more than simply meeting specified requirements and calling it a day. Scrutiny of ESG issues, not just from regulators but also consumers and business partners, is intensifying.

Organizations must be ready to meet strict ESG requirements or face financial penalties, legal action, and reputational damage. Investors and customers now demand transparency, and those that fall behind risk losing access to capital and market opportunities.

### Legal and Regulatory Risk Mitigation

Governments worldwide are expanding ESG compliance laws, shifting from voluntary guidelines to legally enforceable requirements. The EU’s Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) will require large companies to assess and mitigate ESG risks in their supply chains. Germany’s Supply Chain Due Diligence Act (LkSG) fines non-compliant companies up to 2% of global turnover, while the UK Modern Slavery Act mandates annual statements on human rights risks.

Environmental compliance is also tightening. The EU’s [Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM)](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism-cbam/) puts a price on carbon, forcing companies in high-emission industries to cut emissions or pay significant costs. California’s [SB 253](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/california-climate-corporate-data-accountability-act-sb253/) will require companies with over $1 billion in revenue to report scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions, data that many businesses still struggle to collect and verify.

Failure to comply with these regulations leads to more than just fines. Lawsuits, supply chain disruptions, and investor divestment are real consequences for businesses that lack ESG oversight. Companies operating across multiple jurisdictions must navigate overlapping and sometimes conflicting regulatory frameworks, requiring a structured, proactive compliance strategy.

### Investor and Consumer Expectations

ESG performance is now a financial issue. Investment funds managing over $40 trillion in assets integrate ESG factors into decision-making, and failing to disclose ESG risks can limit a company’s access to financing. The Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR) classifies investment funds based on ESG criteria, influencing how capital is allocated. Companies with weak ESG ratings may struggle to secure loans, attract investors, or issue green bonds.

Consumer expectations are also shifting. 78% of global consumers prefer sustainable products, and major retailers increasingly require suppliers to meet ESG standards. Companies in industries like fashion, electronics, and food must demonstrate traceability in their supply chains, comply with deforestation-free sourcing rules, and prove ethical labor practices to avoid losing contracts.

Corporate buyers are also under pressure. Many firms require their suppliers to complete ESG assessments before entering into procurement agreements. Suppliers with poor ESG scores may be excluded from supply chains entirely.

### Competitive Advantage and Reputation Management

Regulatory and stakeholder pressures aside, ESG compliance offers a strategic advantage. Companies with strong ESG policies secure better supply chain relationships, lower financing costs, and improved risk management.

Greenwashing – making misleading sustainability claims – is under heavy scrutiny. The EU Green Claims Directive will impose strict penalties on businesses that can’t substantiate their environmental claims. Regulatory bodies in the US (SEC), UK (CMA), and Australia (ASIC) are investigating companies for deceptive ESG reporting.

Beyond compliance, ESG programs drive operational efficiencies. Companies that optimize energy use, reduce waste, or transition to circular economy models cut costs and improve resilience against resource shortages. ESG-aligned businesses are also better positioned to win government contracts, as many public procurement policies now require suppliers to meet sustainability criteria.

Key ESG Regulations Around the World
------------------------------------

Regulations vary dramatically by region, but they all point to a clear trend: governments are imposing stricter reporting and due diligence requirements. Organizations with international operations must comply with overlapping and sometimes conflicting rules, making global ESG compliance a strategic but necessary challenge to overcome.

### ESG Compliance in the European Union

The EU has the most comprehensive and enforceable ESG compliance framework, covering corporate disclosures, supply chain due diligence, and sustainable finance.

*   **[Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/corporate-sustainability-reporting-directive-csrd/):** Requires detailed ESG disclosures from large EU and non-EU companies operating within the bloc. Expands reporting obligations under European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) and aligns with ISSB and TCFD frameworks.
*   **[Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD)](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/eu-corporate-sustainability-due-diligence-directive-csddd/):** Mandates human rights and environmental due diligence across global supply chains. Large EU firms (and non-EU companies with significant EU revenue) must identify, mitigate, and report on ESG risks in their operations, subsidiaries, and supply chains. Failure to comply can result in fines and liability for damages.
*   **[EU Taxonomy](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/eu-taxonomy/):** Defines what qualifies as a sustainable economic activity, guiding investors and businesses. Companies seeking green financing must align with EU Taxonomy criteria, which determine eligibility for green bonds, loans, and sustainability-linked investments.

Additionally, the [EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR)](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/eu-deforestation-regulation-eudr/) bans imports of deforestation-linked commodities like soy, palm oil, coffee, and rubber unless organizations can prove their supply chains are deforestation-free.

### United States ESG Regulations

The US lacks a single federal ESG law, and with the [SEC climate disclosure rule](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/) on hold, several state-level ESG laws are emerging, including New York’s proposed climate disclosure rules and Colorado’s ESG reporting standards for financial institutions.

*   **[California SB 253](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/california-climate-corporate-data-accountability-act-sb253/):** The Climate Corporate Data Accountability Act mandates scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions reporting for companies with over $1 billion in revenue operating in California. It’s the strictest climate disclosure law in the US and will likely influence federal regulations.
*   **Dodd-Frank Act (Conflict Minerals Rule):** Requires US-listed companies to disclose sourcing of tin, tungsten, tantalum, and gold (3TG) from conflict-affected regions. A key part of supply chain ESG compliance, non-compliance can lead to penalties and reputational risk.

### UK & Canada ESG Regulations

The UK has moved ahead with mandatory ESG disclosures, particularly in climate risk reporting and sustainable finance. Meanwhile, Canada has introduced measures focusing on forced labor risks.

*   [**UK Modern Slavery Act**](https://ecovadis.com/regulations/uk-modern-slavery-act/) requires companies with annual revenues above £36 million to publish an annual slavery and human trafficking statement. Businesses must assess forced labor risks across supply chains, detail mitigation efforts, and disclose actions to prevent human rights abuses.
*   [**Canada’s Bill S-211**](https://resources.ecovadis.com/blog/canada-s-modern-slavery-legislation-supply-chain-transparency-is-required) requires companies to report on forced labor risks in supply chains, similar to the UK Modern Slavery Act. Applies to public and private businesses meeting revenue and operational thresholds.

Challenges in ESG Compliance Implementation
-------------------------------------------

Complying with ESG regulations and standards is no easy process, not least when you’ve got to meet the requirements of several. Regulations are also evolving fast, and organizations must adapt to new requirements while managing data inconsistencies, resource constraints, and supplier compliance gaps.

### Data Collection and Standardization

Many organizations struggle to collect accurate and verifiable ESG data, especially for scope 3 emissions and supply chain risk assessments. Unlike the scope 1 and 2 categories, which are under an organization’s direct control, Scope 3 emissions depend on external suppliers and partners, making reliable data collection difficult.

The lack of standardized ESG reporting frameworks adds another layer of complexity. Organizations must navigate the CSRD, TCFD, GRI, SASB, and ISSB standards, each with different disclosure requirements. This inconsistency makes data comparisons difficult and increases the risk of non-compliance when reporting across multiple jurisdictions.

### Keeping Up with Regulatory Changes

Governments are tightening ESG laws, introducing mandatory reporting, stricter supply chain due diligence, and financial penalties for non-compliance. Those operating in multiple regions must track regulatory updates in real time to avoid falling behind.

Efforts to harmonize global ESG standards, such as the ISSB’s IFRS S1 & S2, aim to create a single global baseline for sustainability reporting. While this reduces long-term complexity, companies must still adjust their reporting processes to align with these new frameworks. Transitioning to these standards requires investments in data collection systems and training compliance teams to interpret evolving requirements.

### Supply Chain Due Diligence

Most ESG risks occur outside an organization’s direct operations, making supply chain due diligence a major compliance challenge. Identifying ESG risks beyond Tier 1 suppliers can be difficult due to limited visibility into subcontractors and raw material sourcing.

Many suppliers, particularly in emerging markets, lack the resources or expertise to meet ESG compliance standards. Large companies are expected to audit, train, and support suppliers in meeting due diligence requirements, which can be resource-intensive. Non-compliant suppliers pose legal, financial, and reputational risks, and failing to enforce ESG standards in the supply chain can result in penalties under CSDDD or the UK Modern Slavery Act.

Best Practices for ESG Compliance
---------------------------------

ESG compliance requires a structured approach that integrates risk management, data collection, governance, and supplier oversight. Businesses that implement clear policies, leverage technology, and engage suppliers effectively are better positioned to meet regulatory requirements and stakeholder expectations.

### Establishing an ESG Compliance Framework

This begins with identifying material risks and aligning corporate policies with global standards.

*   Materiality assessments help determine which ESG issues pose the greatest financial, operational, and reputational risks. This process ensures that businesses prioritize compliance efforts where they matter most.
*   Organizations should align with recognized ESG reporting frameworks like GRI, TCFD, SASB, and ISSB. These standards provide structured reporting guidelines that meet investor and regulatory expectations.
*   Organizations must embed ESG compliance into corporate strategy, ensuring that executives, legal teams, and procurement managers work together to integrate sustainability into operations.

### Strengthening Governance and Reporting Practices

ESG compliance requires clear accountability and independent verification to ensure credibility and prevent greenwashing risks.

*   Assigning a Chief Sustainability Officer (CSO) or ESG Compliance Lead ensures ESG policies are enforced across departments and compliance efforts are coordinated with financial and legal teams.
*   Third-party ESG audits provide independent verification of sustainability reports, supply chain due diligence, and emissions tracking. Many regulations, including the CSRD and California SB 253, require external assurance for reported data.
*   Board oversight and executive incentives linked to ESG performance help drive accountability. Organizations that tie executive compensation to ESG goals are more likely to implement effective compliance measures.

### Supplier Engagement and Risk Management

As we highlighted earlier, many ESG compliance risks come from supplier operations. This makes supplier engagement an important component of any ESG compliance strategy.

*   Establishing Supplier Codes of Conduct aligned with the CSDDD, UK Modern Slavery Act, and forced labor laws sets clear ESG expectations for vendors. These codes should include minimum labor standards, environmental targets, and ethical sourcing requirements.
*   Companies should train suppliers on ESG compliance, providing capacity-building programs and sustainability workshops to help smaller vendors improve environmental and human rights practices.
*   Supplier ESG performance assessments – including on-site audits, self-assessments, and [third-party evaluations](https://ecovadis.com/solutions/ratings/) – help businesses identify high-risk suppliers and take corrective actions before violations occur.

Managing ESG compliance is complex, but it doesn’t have to slow you down. EcoVadis provides trusted ESG ratings, supplier risk mapping and AI-driven analytics to help you meet global regulations. Five Best Practices to Ensure ESG Compliance
============================================

What started as a largely voluntary box-ticking exercise has shifted into a priority for investors globally, with ESG compliance coming under intense scrutiny as it represents a firm’s commitment to proper risk management and sustainable investing. However, maintaining a vigilant eye on compliance can be a complex undertaking, requiring a deep understanding of regulatory requirements and continuous monitoring of a firm’s operations.

What is ESG Compliance?
-----------------------

ESG compliance involves adhering to guidelines and regulations that govern environmental, social, and governance (ESG) standards as mandated by global regulatory bodies and frameworks. However, compliance is more than fulfilling a legal obligation — it’s also about taking proactive measures toward sustainability, and ensuring organizations have responsible and ethical business practices that can bring positive impact and change to the communities they operate in.

Ensuring ESG Compliance in Private Markets
------------------------------------------

### 1. Start Early and Set the Groundwork for Compliance

Being motivated to start is a great first step, but it’s critical to remember that ensuring ESG compliance takes more than just intention. Building disclosure reports involves materiality assessments, stakeholder buy-in, collecting data from your portfolio companies, and so much more — all processes that cannot be done overnight.

Start by identifying the compliance frameworks your organization needs to report to, before understanding the depth of their disclosure requirements. For example, the [Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD)](https://www.fsb-tcfd.org/about/) is especially pertinent to private markets, as it offers a structured framework for assessing and disclosing climate-related financial risks and opportunities of portfolio companies. You can then align those requirements with your goals, whether it be regulatory or an industry-specific objective.

Many organizations are also [more prepared to comply](https://www.novata.com/resources/blog/how-csrd-aligns-with-existing-esg-disclosures/) than they realize. In 2023, the European Financial Reporting Advisory Group (EFRAG), the advisory body responsible for drafting the CSRD’s reporting standards, [released a joint statement](https://efrag.org/news/public-444/EFRAG-GRI-Joint-statement-of-interoperability-) with the International Sustainability Standard Board (ISSB) and the Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) to confirm a high level of interoperability between their respective standards.

Not only does this significantly reduce redundancies and reporting burdens, but companies can also leverage these synergies to analyze gaps and develop robust governance processes to ensure they are better prepared to meet reporting requirements in the future.

### 2. Shift From Stakeholder Management to Stakeholder Engagement

ESG compliance isn’t just about numbers and data — it’s also a commitment to understanding and responding to the needs and concerns of stakeholders. Rather than viewing sustainability as a siloed responsibility, ESG leaders should be having active dialogues with internal and external stakeholders to understand their expectations and concerns around ESG, and how they can tailor their approach accordingly to drive further buy-in.

For instance, understanding investor expectations can lead to capital allocation to more responsible investments, while employee insights can encourage improvements in [diversity, equity, and inclusion (DE&I) initiatives](https://learn-more.novata.com/hubfs/231031-Novata-Diversity-Insights.pdf?_gl=1*m09yqd*_ga*MTE3MzgxMDU4NC4xNzAwNjQyNzIy*_ga_TL2MCK69X7*MTcxMjYzMTEzNS44Ni4xLjE3MTI2MzEyNDEuNDIuMC4w). These not only demonstrate a genuine commitment to ESG but can also bolster stakeholder relationships and further foster a culture of trust, transparency, and accountability.

Your stakeholders are the stewards of your company’s values, and implementing a collaborative, inclusive approach to stakeholder engagement can truly transform how ESG is integrated and perceived across the organization.

### 3. Empower Your Portfolio Companies

Obtaining high-quality, accurate ESG data from portfolio companies has both been a priority and a challenge for GPs, with [71% citing](https://securities.cib.bnpparibas/global-esg-survey-2023/global-esg-survey-2023-report/) “incomplete and inconsistent” data as a barrier to ESG investing. Given how quality data is inextricably linked to compliance, firms need to engage and empower their portfolio companies in order to get the data they need to reliably track ESG performance and progress.

Establish the challenges faced by your portfolio companies, such as a lack of ESG maturity or limited resources, and provide them with the necessary tools, training, and resources to overcome these gaps. This might include anything from training sessions to adopting an [end-to-end ESG data collection platform](https://www.novata.com/resources/blog/considerations-for-choosing-esg-data-software/) to simplify metric selection and data analysis. The goal here isn’t to further burden your portfolio companies but rather to support them in their reporting efforts without disrupting their day-to-day operations.

### 4. Don’t Be Afraid To Lean on Peers and Seek Support

Ensuring ESG compliance can be an arduous journey alone. If you’re facing major roadblocks, be empowered to lean on industry peers and seek support from industry associations, advisory bodies, or even [external ESG experts](https://www.novata.com/esg-services/).

Collaborating on best practices, participating in ESG forums, and sharing resources can be mutually beneficial. Not only will you gain insights and tips from others’ successes and learnings, but it also contributes to industry-wide advances in ESG reporting and compliance. Such collective knowledge can be invaluable in shaping your firm’s compliance strategy and ensuring that you’re at the forefront of meaningful ESG action.

### 5. Adopt a Compliance Checklist

Having an ESG compliance checklist can put your current ESG practices and performance into perspective while acting as a guideline to maximize compliance efforts. What goes into your checklist should ideally align with your specific organizational goals, but below are a few essential steps that should be included:

*   Alignment with regulatory frameworks that your firm needs to adhere to
*   ESG performance assessment against set targets to track progress
*   A review of ESG risk management strategies based on regulatory changes, operational feedback, and other emerging trends
*   A standardized approach to data collection
*   Discussions with stakeholders to obtain feedback and suggestions on key ESG issues
*   Seeking third-party verification to deliver reasonable assurance of your ESG performance and data

The Road to Compliance Is Not Easy, but It Needs To Be Done
-----------------------------------------------------------

ESG compliance often comes naturally as a byproduct of a firm’s high governance standards and genuine commitment to sustainability. Ensuring compliance takes plenty of time and preparation, especially for firms disclosing for the very first time.

Many firms struggle with the collection of accurate, reliable ESG data, which are the building blocks for ESG compliance. Novata helps streamline the data collection process and makes it easy to gain actionable insights from the data. Learn more about [how Novata can help](https://www.novata.com/learn-more/).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Develop comprehensive ESG policies that align with regulatory frameworks and stakeholder expectations. This foundation ensures accountability and guides corporate actions toward sustainability objectives.
Implement frequent ESG audits to assess compliance and identify areas for improvement. Regular evaluations help mitigate risks and enhance transparency in sustainability practices.
Adopt reliable data collection methods and reporting frameworks. Accurate disclosures build stakeholder trust and meet legal obligations, demonstrating the company's commitment to ESG accountability.
Foster open communication with stakeholders to understand their concerns and expectations. Engaging stakeholders helps align ESG initiatives with community needs and enhances corporate reputation.
Stay updated on evolving ESG regulations and compliance requirements. Proactive monitoring ensures timely adaptation to new standards, minimizing legal risks and maintaining competitive advantage.
